game_name: Street Fighter II: The World Warrior

design_philosophy.core_loop: One-on-one close-quarters combat with command-based special moves, combo system, and six-button configuration. Player must drain opponent's health bar or maintain advantage when timer expires across best-of-three rounds.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Head-to-head competitive skill expression: memorizing character-specific command inputs, executing frame-perfect combos, reading opponent spacing, and managing offense/defense positioning.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Master a unique fighting character with distinct special moves and fighting style; prove dominance against increasingly skilled international opponents; climb the ranking ladder in single-player arcade campaign.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Shift from personal high-score competition to direct head-to-head adversarial play. Each of 8 playable characters has intentionally asymmetric move sets (Ryu's Shoryuken, Blanka's electrical attacks, Dhalsim's yoga fire, Zangief's grapples) forcing players to master character-specific execution patterns rather than universal strategies. Regional versions deliberately altered character profiles and win quotes to localize personality and narrative context for international audiences.

difficulty.continues_system: Standard arcade continue structure; single-player progression allows restart after defeat
difficulty.checkpointing: Non-linear opponent selection via world map after every match; bonus stages interleaved after every third match (car/barrels/oil drums destruction for points)
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects difficulty implicitly through opponent selection order. Arcade AI scales opponent skill predictably. Two-player mode allows human players to calibrate challenge directly against each other.

progression.unlock_mechanism: World map navigation determines geographic opponent order; hidden characters (Four Kings: M. Bison/Balrog/Vega/Sagat) unlocked by reaching final campaign stages. Single-player campaign progresses through 10 predetermined opponents with bonus stage interruptions.
replayability_hooks: 8 distinct playable characters with asymmetric move sets encourage mastery of different fighting styles
Two-player competitive mode shifts focus from AI predictability to human opponent unpredictability
Character-specific win quotes and profiles (expanded in Japanese version) reward learning all characters
Bonus stages provide skill-based score optimization opportunities
World map traversal creates perception of global tournament structure

world_building_techniques: Character profile cards with likes/dislikes and biographical snippets (expanded detail in Japanese version: Ryu pursuing martial mastery, Guile seeking Nash's revenge, Chun-Li investigating father's disappearance, Dhalsim fighting for orphans)
Geographic world map showing international tournament locations
Win quotes tailored per character with personality variance (Japanese version has substantially more dialogue reflecting individual motivations)

Character design visual language communicates cultural identity and fighting discipline (sumo wrestler E. Honda's formal dignity, jungle-raised Blanka's primal aggression, Soviet wrestler Zangief's cold pragmatism)

boss_design.philosophy: Four hidden bosses (Four Kings: M. Bison/Balrog/Vega/Sagat) act as campaign capstone after defeating 10 standard opponents. Boss encounters appear predetermined on world map progression rather than adaptive difficulty scaling. Each boss character maintains the asymmetric moveset philosophy of standard characters but with enhanced execution demands and aggressive AI patterns.
notable_bosses: M. Bison (Dictator) - Final boss; powerful special moves and aggression
Balrog/Vega (Claw) - Defensive, mobile boss with projectile harassment
Sagat (Emperor) - Tall character with long-range pressure tools
Balrog (Boxer) - Aggressive rushdown offense (character name/role varied by region)

faction_indicators: Geographically anchored character identities: Japan (Ryu, Ken, E. Honda), Brazil (Blanka), USA (Guile), China (Chun-Li), Russia (Zangief), India (Dhalsim) create implicit cultural representation and regional pride
Personality archetypes in win quotes: Ryu as humble martial artist, Ken as cocky rival, E. Honda as boastful traditionalist, Guile as duty-bound soldier, Chun-Li as determined detective
Hidden Four Kings represent shadow organization (Shadoloo) hierarchy introducing villain faction narrative
danger_signals: M. Bison/Vega as dictator antagonist figure commanding criminal organization
Balrog's name/character identity swapped between regions (Boxer vs. Claw) suggesting censorship of violent archetype
Chun-Li's search for missing father thematizes authoritarian kidnapping/disappearance as threat
environmental_storytelling: World map geography legitimizes tournament as global event spanning continents
Bonus stage destruction mechanics (car/barrels/drums) contextualize fighting prowess through environmental impact
Character select screen visual presentation emphasizes international diversity and cultural distinction
Japanese version character profiles establish social context (Dhalsim's orphanage, Guile's military loyalty, Chun-Li's law enforcement investigation) missing from international versions

sources_used: tcrf - Street Fighter II: The World Warrior (SNES) technical documentation, character profiles (Japanese/international variants), win quotes comparative analysis, regional differences

wikipedia - Street Fighter II article: gameplay mechanics, design credits (Yoshiki Okamoto producer, Akira Nishitani director), arcade/console release dates, commercial impact data, character roster overview
